- Cost and cleanup: infra-cost-ops ensures ephemeral resource reclamation and TTL policies are enforced.

Metrics and dashboards (minimum):
- Canary health dashboard (per-run): pod readiness, device enumeration count, p50/p95 inference latency, checkpoint success rate, egress throughput, terraform drift rate.
- Long-term: flakiness rate per operator commit, time-to-detect, time-to-recover.

Troubleshooting quick checklist:
- If device count is 0: check node-driver-manager logs -> check kernel module presence -> verify driver package digest matches pinned artifact.
- If pod crashloops after upgrade: capture event stream (kubectl describe pod) and current image digests; try redeploy to fresh ephemeral node to isolate node-level vs operator-level issues.
- If egress shaping fails: validate proxy config, token expiry for replayed traces, and NAT gateway quotas.

Retention and cost controls:
- Default TTL for ephemeral testbeds: 6 hours. Pipeline must explicit-extend TTL for long debug sessions.
- Enforce per-pipeline budget caps and alert infra-cost-ops when a single run would exceed $X (configurable in pipeline variables).

Operational knobs and toggles (examples):
- pipeline.variable:CANARY_DURATION -> how long staging canary runs (default 24h)
- pipeline.flag:FAIL_ON_LATENCY -> whether to treat latency regressions as hard failures in CI (default true)
- infra.iam:ci-test-runner-role -> least privilege role used to run Terraform and create clusters

Appendix: Example pipeline steps (simplified YAML snippet):

1) Checkout + lint
2) Build operator image (CI registry digest pinned)
3) Terraform apply (ci workspace) -> export kubeconfig to secret
4) Helm deploy operator (image digest)
5) Run smoke suite (kubectl run samples + capture metrics)
6) Run driver-mismatch + KV + egress tests (batch)
7) Collect artifacts (logs, device traces, perf snapshots) -> upload to minio store
8) Evaluate gates (script returns pass/fail, records to dashboard)
9) If pass, tag image as canary and trigger staging promotion job
10) Post-cleanup: terraform destroy or schedule TTL-based destroy

References and related reading:
- See canary provisioning pipeline for example picklists (related pages).
- Operator development guidelines (internal repo) for build and image pinning.

Contact and escalation:
- Primary owner: Maya Chen (maya@redwood.example.com)
- Secondary: Luis Ortega (luis@redwood.example.com)
- Oncall: infra-sre rotation (pager details in team wiki)

Change log:
- 2024-06-10: Initial draft and testbed design (Maya Chen)
- 2024-11-02: Added Terraform drift-check pattern and TTL defaults (review: Priya Raman)
- 2025-02-14: Updated canary thresholds and automated rollback steps (review: Ethan Park)

Document maintenance: Review this page quarterly or when the operator build/promotion flow changes.

title: Multi-tenant Burst Orchestration and Network SLA Playbook
preview:
Summary:
This playbook describes how Redwood Inference handles short-term multi-tenant bursts of GPU demand while preserving per-tenant network SLAs and overall cluster stability. It documents policy decisions, IAC patterns, CI/CD rollout guidance, operational runbooks for incidents, and examples (Terraform, CLI) for on-call and SRE teams.

Audience:
- SREs and infra engineers who operate GPU clusters and tenant networking
- Platform engineers implementing autoscalers and egress shaping
- Solutions engineers onboarding tenants with bursty workloads

Goals and constraints:
- Allow safe, bounded bursting from tenants up to a cluster-wide QPS/Token budget without violating network egress and latency SLAs.
- Preserve tail latency SLOs for inference (p95 token latency <= 120ms for standard tier).
- Minimize preemptions and warm pool churn.
- Ensure predictable billing signals and clear tenant backpressure behaviors.

Key components (service names):
- gpu-pool-manager: manages pool membership, warm pools, and padding.
- tenant-allocator: maps tenant requests to pools and enforces per-tenant credits.
- spot-fallback-autoscaler: scales fallback pools when spot preemption probability rises.
- inference-router: routes requests to model shards and handles prefix caching.
- egress-shaper: per-tenant network shaper that enforces egress bandwidth SLAs and burst tokens.
- burst-bridge: temporary burst routing path for short-lived requests to dedicated burst pools.

Architectural overview (short):
When a tenant request is accepted, tenant-allocator checks the tenant's burst budget and egress credits. If the tenant is within sustained usage, inference-router routes normally. If a sudden rate increase is detected (defined below), the request can be admitted to a burst-bridge pool if:
1) gpu-pool-manager reports available burst slots in warm-pool margins, and
2) egress-shaper can provide burst tokens without breaking tenant or cluster SLAs.

Policy definitions and thresholds:
- Burst window: 60s rolling.
- Burst eligibility: tenant sustained rate increase > 5x baseline OR absolute tokens/s > 50k for standard tenants.
- Per-tenant burst cap: 20% of tenant's committed capacity or 2 GPU-equivalent slots, whichever is larger.
- Cluster burst reservoir: at most 10% of total GPU pool can be used for cross-tenant bursts.
- Egress burst token size: 5 MB/s per token, up to 3 tokens per tenant for 30s.

SLOs and alert thresholds (operational):
- Scheduling success rate (5m window) >= 99.5%. Alert if < 99.0% for 3 consecutive 1m windows.
- Eviction rate (1h) < 0.5% of active jobs. Alert if > 1.0%.
- Egress tail latency p95 (per-tenant) <= 50ms for control-plane calls. Alert if p95 > 100ms for 5m.
- Burst token exhaustion: Pager if more than 5 tenants simultaneously hit burst caps for >120s.

Terraform / IaC pattern (summary):
- Each tenant allocation is represented by a tenant-binding resource: tenant_binding { tenant_id, committed_gpus, priority_class }
- gpu_pool module exports: pool_id, warm_slots, reserved_slots, burst_reservoir (float 0-1).
- egress_shaper module exports per-tenant shaper policer with token bucket parameters.

Example Terraform snippet (annotated):
resource \"redwood_gpu_pool\" \"north_east_inference\" {
  name = \"pool-ne-inference\"
  node_count = 40
  warm_slots = 6
  reserved_slots = 4
  burst_reservoir = 0.10 # 10% of pool available for cross-tenant bursts
}

resource \"redwood_tenant_binding\" \"acme_prod\" {
  tenant_id = \"acme-prod\"
  committed_gpus = 2
  priority_class = \"standard\"
  egress_shaper = module.egress_shaper_acme.id
}

CI/CD and rollout guidance:
- All changes to autoscaler or egress-shaper must go through the autoscaler CI pipeline: git branch -> ci/static-checks -> integration-e2e with a 100-tenant synthetic workload -> canary rollout to 5% of clusters -> gradual promotion.
- Use the \"burst-sim\" integration job to run load-tests that emulate 50% of tenants sending 10x baseline for 90s.
- Canary gating checks: scheduling success >= 99.7%, eviction rate <= 0.3%, network egress pacing OK.

Runbook: On-call steps for \"Burst-induced scheduling failures\" pager
1) Acknowledge pager and post to #oncall-gpu with incident-id and short summary.
2) Run: kubectl get pods -n inference | grep CrashLoopBackOff and check gpu-pool-manager logs: 
   kubectl -n infra logs deploy/gpu-pool-manager -c manager --since=15m | tail -n 200
3) Check real-time metrics:
   - grafana dashboard: cluster/gpu/burst-overview (5m)
   - query: sum(rate(scheduler_success_total[1m])) by (pool)
4) If scheduling success < threshold, execute mitigation:
   a) Temporarily reduce cross-tenant burst reservoir: 
      curl -X POST https://controlplane/api/v1/pools/{pool_id}/mutate -d '{\"burst_reservoir\":0.02}' -u deploy-bot-token
   b) Scale up warm-pools for critical pools (if capacity exists): 
      terraform apply -target=module.gpu_pool[\"pool-ne-inference\"] -var=warm_slots=10
5) If egress shaping shows bursts saturating links, add per-tenant throttles via egress-shaper: 
   curl -X POST https://controlplane/api/v1/egress/{tenant_id}/set -d '{\"tokens_per_minute\":3,\"token_size_mb\":5}'
6) After stabilization, open an incident tracker and attach timeline + CI artifacts.

Incident postmortem checklist (short):
- Who paged? Which tenant caused the initial spike?
- Which pools were affected and what were the immediate mitigations?
- Confirm terraform diffs used to modify pools and retain apply logs.
- Create action items: adjust burst caps, increase warm-pool target, or tune egress token size.

Automation and observability (dashboards/queries):
- Dashboards: cluster/gpu/burst-overview, tenant/egress/token-usage, scheduler/latency-p95-by-pool, autoscaler/warm-pool-fill.
- Key queries (PromQL):
  * Scheduling success rate: sum(rate(scheduler_success_total[1m])) / sum(rate(scheduler_attempt_total[1m]))
  * Burst reservoir utilization: sum(gpu_burst_used) / sum(gpu_burst_reservoir)
  * Tenant token burn: increase(egress_tokens_consumed_total[1m]) by (tenant_id)

Tenant onboarding checklist for burst-prone customers:
1) Review expected QPS and token/sec profile with solutions engineering.
2) Approve committed capacity and burst allowance in tenant_binding.
3) Provide egress requirements (peak MB/s and acceptable burst duration).
4) Run a staged ramp test using burst-sim and sign off in a joint runbook session.

Operational knobs and why they exist (short):
- warm_slots: reduces cold-start latency and avoids preemptions.
- reserved_slots: guarantees committed capacity for paying tenants.
- burst_reservoir: allows short-term elasticity balanced across tenants.
- egress_shaper tokens: protect shared networking and enforce billing fairness.

Safeguards and escalation policies:
- Governance: any change to cluster-wide burst_reservoir requires SRE+InfraEng approval and a recorded Terraform PR.
- Emergency rollback: control-plane supports \"fast-rollback\" for pool mutates. Use only if cluster stability is at risk.
- Post-deploy audits: automation runs a diff-check every 6 hours comparing intended terraform state and control-plane state; mismatches open a non-blocking alert.

Open items and proposals:
- Evaluate per-tenant predictive credit using short-term ML signals from workload-shape predictions (PoC Q3 2025).
- Implement network-aware scheduling so that tenants with high egress locality are favored during bursts.

Owners and contacts:
- Primary owner: Maya R. Chen (infra-platform) 
- Secondary: Diego Alvarez (sre)
- Pager channel: #oncall-gpu
- Runbook doc link: eng-infra/runbooks/preflight-gpu-deployment-validation-and-rollback-playbook

Appendix A: Example CLI debugging commands
- Check burst reservoir utilization: 
  curl -s https://controlplane/api/v1/pools/pool-ne-inference/metrics | jq '.burst_reservoir'
- List tenants currently using burst slots: 
  curl -s https://controlplane/api/v1/burst/allocations | jq '.[] | {tenant_id, slots}'

Appendix B: Quick Terraform migration checklist
1) Create feature branch and modify module variables (warm_slots, burst_reservoir).
2) Run terraform plan with -out and run automated static checks.
3) Trigger integration-e2e (burst-sim) pipeline.
4) After green, open PR with required reviewers (SRE + Infra + Security if networking changed).
5) Apply to canary clusters first, monitor 30m, then promote.

Change log:
- 2024-07-15: Created initial draft outlining policy and components.
- 2024-10-01: Added egress token parameters and CI canary gating steps.
- 2025-02-12: Clarified SLO thresholds and added terraform quick rollback guidance.

title: gpu-latency-regression-investigation-playbook
preview:
Overview:

This runbook defines a repeatable investigation and mitigation workflow for sudden latency regressions observed in GPU-backed inference flows. It targets production inference routes served by inference-router, model-proxy, batcher, kvcache-service, and gpu-scheduler. The goal is to restore latency SLOs quickly, identify root cause, and produce follow-up remediation that prevents recurrence.

Scope and audience:
- Audience: oncall infra engineers, serving-runtime owners, SRE rotation.
- Scope: cross-service latency regressions that impact P50/P95/P99 of token-generation or request-latency for interactive routes. Does NOT cover upstream model quality regressions (use product-evals) or planned maintenance windows.

SLOs and alerting thresholds (summary):
- Short interactive routes (<= 256 tokens): p50 < 60ms, p95 < 150ms, p99 < 300ms.
- Long streaming (>= 512 tokens): p50 < 120ms, p95 < 350ms, p99 < 750ms.
- Alert triggers (automatic):
  - LAT-ALERT-01: sustained p95 > SLO for 5m OR p99 > 2x SLO for 2m.
  - LAT-ALERT-02: inbound request queue length > 50 for 3m.
  - LAT-ALERT-03: GPU kernel stalls (kernel_failure_rate > 0.5%) or OOMs > 3/min.

Primary signals and dashboards to consult (ordered):
1) Inference latency dashboard (Grafana): per-route p50/p95/p99, tokens-per-request, model variant.
2) Router metrics: inference-router.latency_ms{route,region,model}.
3) Batcher queue metrics: batcher.queue_length, batcher.merge_latency_ms.
4) KV cache hit ratio: kvcache.hit_rate by model-version and route.
5) GPU node metrics: gpu.utilization, gpu.mem.used, gpu.mem.free, pci-errors, gpu_temperature.
6) Pod/container OOMKilled, restart_count, and kernel logs (dmesg).
7) Network indicators: pod_network_tx_err, pod_network_rx_err, inter-region p99 RTT.
8) Downstream/backpressure: job-queue-lengths and kafka consumer lag (if used).

Key PromQL queries (copy/paste):
- p95 latency for route X:
  histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(inference_request_duration_seconds_bucket{route=\"__ROUTE__\"}[5m])) by (le))
- batcher queue length:
  sum(batcher_queue_length{model=~\"__MODEL_REGEX__\"})
- kv cache hit rate (percent):
  100 * sum(rate(kvcache_hits_total[5m])) / (sum(rate(kvcache_hits_total[5m])) + sum(rate(kvcache_misses_total[5m])))
- GPU OOMs per minute:
  increase(kube_pod_container_status_last_termination_reason{reason=\"OOMKilled\"}[5m])

Immediate triage checklist (first 10 minutes):
1) Confirm alert and scope: which routes, regions, and models are affected.
2) Check global telemetry: look at last 15 minutes on latency dashboard and cluster-wide GPU alerts.
3) Verify if change was deployed in the last 30–60 minutes: check CI/CD deployment history (Buildkite pipeline IDs) and recent model-rollout events.
4) Check batcher queue length and merge latency. If queue_length > 30 or merge_latency > 200ms, treat as batching backlog.
5) Check kvcache hit rate drop. If hit_rate fell > 20 percentage points, suspect cache eviction or version mismatch.
6) Inspect GPU node metrics for throttling, memory pressure, or high temperature.
7) Scan logs for recent error spikes: model-proxy 5xx spikes, OOM errors, GPU driver messages.
8) If network errors or inter-region links show increased RTT/pkt-loss, consider routing fallback.
9) Notify #infra-oncall with a short summary including affected routes, suspected subsystem, and preliminary action.
10) If no clear immediate cause, escalate to eng-sre and owners of serving-runtime and model-onboarding.

Mitigation decision matrix (quick guide):
- Cause: Batcher backlog or poor merging 
  Immediate action: temporarily reduce max_batch_size to 70% of normal for affected worker pools or scale batcher replicas + enable warm-pool.
  Rationale: reduces merge latency and evens processing at the cost of throughput.

- Cause: KV cache thrashing / cold-starts
  Immediate action: promote cache TTL from 30s to 120s for affected model-versions, or pin hot prefixes to sticky warm nodes (use feature: kvcache.hotpin=true).
  Rationale: restores hit rate and reduces repeated decode costs.

- Cause: Model rollout/perf regression
  Immediate action: rollback to previous model variant via model-router canary rollback API: POST /api/v1/model-router/rollbacks {\"route\":\"__ROUTE__\",\"to_version\":\"vX\"}
  Rationale: restore known-good inference performance while investigating model-specific changes.

- Cause: GPU node instability (OOMs/driver errors)
  Immediate action: cordon and drain affected nodes, spin up replacement nodes in hetero-warm-pool, and reduce weight of pool in gpu-scheduler to zero for draining nodes.
  Commands: kubectl cordon <node>; kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data
  Rationale: remove noisy hardware from serving and restore capacity with healthy nodes.

- Cause: Network degradation (inter-region or overlay)
  Immediate action: switch affected routes to local-region-only routing policy, enable fallback to cached small models, and open ticket with cloud-networking for BGP/peering investigation.

Step-by-step mitigation (recommended order):
1) If batcher backlog: increase batcher replicas by +30% for affected region and lower max_batch_size in flight. Monitor queue length.
2) If cache drop: adjust kvcache TTL and rehydrate prefixes with warmup job (run warmup-runner --route __ROUTE__ --seed-keys 200).
3) If model suspect: trigger immediate rollback to previous model variant (see rollback API above) and mark rollout as halted in model-onboarding.
4) If GPU node problem: cordon/drain affected nodes, attach replacement nodes using gpu-autoscaler template.
5) If network: re-route traffic, mark incident with cloud-networking, and apply temporary geo-blocking for noisy paths.

Operational commands and snippets (copy/paste safe examples):
- Check recent deployments (Buildkite): bk list-builds --pipeline inference-deploy --branch main --limit 10
- Rollback model via curl:
  curl -X POST -H \"Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN\" -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" https://controlplane.redux.company/api/v1/model-router/rollbacks -d '{\"route\":\"chat-standard\",\"to_version\":\"v3.2.1\"}'
- Promote kvcache TTL via admin API:
  curl -X PATCH https://controlplane.redux.company/api/v1/kvcache/config -d '{\"route\":\"chat-standard\",\"ttl_seconds\":120}'
- Scale batcher deployment (k8s):
  kubectl -n serving scale deploy batcher-chat-standard --replicas=12

Escalation and communications:
- Triage update cadence: post initial summary in #infra-oncall within 10 minutes, then updates every 15 minutes until stabilized.
- If not resolved within 30 minutes: page on-call ENG-SRE lead, and open an incident in the incident tracker (use /incidents create).
- External customer impact: notify customer-success if more than two enterprise customers are reporting increased latency or SLA breaches.

Post-incident actions (within 72 hours):
1) Assemble a short RCA document and timeline in the incident tracker. Include relevant graphs: p95/p99 before, during, after; batcher queue length; kvcache hit rate; GPU node events.
2) Run a regression test matrix: replay representative workloads against a staging cluster with the suspect model or build.
3) If root cause is infra: raise an ADR or IaC change proposal (terraform change) to harden capacity, e.g., adjust node warm-pool sizing or add admission-controller guardrails.
4) Update runbook: add new detection rules or automated mitigations discovered during the incident.
5) Follow up action owner: assign remediation story to eng-infra backlog with priority P1/P2.

Testing this runbook (runbook game-day):
- Quarterly runbook drills: simulate a batcher backlog scenario by injecting synthetic traffic with high tokens-per-request and measure detection-to-mitigation time.
- Validation checklist: ensure rollback endpoints and admin APIs accept test tokens, confirm safe dry-run of cordon/drain commands, and verify Buildkite pipeline audit logs are available.

Appendix A — Alert table (config snippet):
| Alert ID | PromQL snippet | Trigger | Owner | Runbook action |
| LAT-ALERT-01 | histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(inference_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le,route)) > SLO | p95 breach 5m | eng-sre | follow triage checklist |
| LAT-ALERT-02 | sum(batcher_queue_length) > 50 | queue > 50 for 3m | serving-runtime | scale batcher / reduce max_batch |
| LAT-ALERT-03 | increase(kube_pod_container_status_last_termination_reason{reason=\"OOMKilled\"}[5m]) > 3 | >3 OOMs in 5m | infra-node-ops | cordon/drain nodes |

Appendix B — Owners and contacts:
- oncall Slack: #infra-oncall (primary channel)
- SRE lead pager: eng-sre-lead@oncall.pagerduty.com
- Serving runtime owner: @diego.alvarez (Diego Alvarez)
- Model onboarding: @samantha.green (Samantha Green)

Appendix C — Related runbooks and pages:

- kv-cache-reconstruction-and-eviction-resilience-runbook

text:
Overview:

This runbook defines a repeatable investigation and mitigation workflow for sudden latency regressions observed in GPU-backed inference flows. It targets production inference routes served by inference-router, model-proxy, batcher, kvcache-service, and gpu-scheduler. The goal is to restore latency SLOs quickly, identify root cause, and produce follow-up remediation that prevents recurrence.

Scope and audience:
- Audience: oncall infra engineers, serving-runtime owners, SRE rotation.
- Scope: cross-service latency regressions that impact P50/P95/P99 of token-generation or request-latency for interactive routes. Does NOT cover upstream model quality regressions (use product-evals) or planned maintenance windows.

SLOs and alerting thresholds (summary):
- Short interactive routes (<= 256 tokens): p50 < 60ms, p95 < 150ms, p99 < 300ms.
- Long streaming (>= 512 tokens): p50 < 120ms, p95 < 350ms, p99 < 750ms.
- Alert triggers (automatic):
  - LAT-ALERT-01: sustained p95 > SLO for 5m OR p99 > 2x SLO for 2m.
  - LAT-ALERT-02: inbound request queue length > 50 for 3m.
  - LAT-ALERT-03: GPU kernel stalls (kernel_failure_rate > 0.5%) or OOMs > 3/min.

Primary signals and dashboards to consult (ordered):
1) Inference latency dashboard (Grafana): per-route p50/p95/p99, tokens-per-request, model variant.
2) Router metrics: inference-router.latency_ms{route,region,model}.
3) Batcher queue metrics: batcher.queue_length, batcher.merge_latency_ms.
4) KV cache hit ratio: kvcache.hit_rate by model-version and route.
5) GPU node metrics: gpu.utilization, gpu.mem.used, gpu.mem.free, pci-errors, gpu_temperature.
6) Pod/container OOMKilled, restart_count, and kernel logs (dmesg).
7) Network indicators: pod_network_tx_err, pod_network_rx_err, inter-region p99 RTT.
8) Downstream/backpressure: job-queue-lengths and kafka consumer lag (if used).

Key PromQL queries (copy/paste):
- p95 latency for route X:
  histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(inference_request_duration_seconds_bucket{route=\"__ROUTE__\"}[5m])) by (le))
- batcher queue length:
  sum(batcher_queue_length{model=~\"__MODEL_REGEX__\"})
- kv cache hit rate (percent):
  100 * sum(rate(kvcache_hits_total[5m])) / (sum(rate(kvcache_hits_total[5m])) + sum(rate(kvcache_misses_total[5m])))
- GPU OOMs per minute:
  increase(kube_pod_container_status_last_termination_reason{reason=\"OOMKilled\"}[5m])

Immediate triage checklist (first 10 minutes):
1) Confirm alert and scope: which routes, regions, and models are affected.
2) Check global telemetry: look at last 15 minutes on latency dashboard and cluster-wide GPU alerts.
3) Verify if change was deployed in the last 30–60 minutes: check CI/CD deployment history (Buildkite pipeline IDs) and recent model-rollout events.
4) Check batcher queue length and merge latency. If queue_length > 30 or merge_latency > 200ms, treat as batching backlog.
5) Check kvcache hit rate drop. If hit_rate fell > 20 percentage points, suspect cache eviction or version mismatch.
6) Inspect GPU node metrics for throttling, memory pressure, or high temperature.
7) Scan logs for recent error spikes: model-proxy 5xx spikes, OOM errors, GPU driver messages.
8) If network errors or inter-region links show increased RTT/pkt-loss, consider routing fallback.
9) Notify #infra-oncall with a short summary including affected routes, suspected subsystem, and preliminary action.
10) If no clear immediate cause, escalate to eng-sre and owners of serving-runtime and model-onboarding.

Mitigation decision matrix (quick guide):
- Cause: Batcher backlog or poor merging 
  Immediate action: temporarily reduce max_batch_size to 70% of normal for affected worker pools or scale batcher replicas + enable warm-pool.
  Rationale: reduces merge latency and evens processing at the cost of throughput.

- Cause: KV cache thrashing / cold-starts
  Immediate action: promote cache TTL from 30s to 120s for affected model-versions, or pin hot prefixes to sticky warm nodes (use feature: kvcache.hotpin=true).
  Rationale: restores hit rate and reduces repeated decode costs.

- Cause: Model rollout/perf regression
  Immediate action: rollback to previous model variant via model-router canary rollback API: POST /api/v1/model-router/rollbacks {\"route\":\"__ROUTE__\",\"to_version\":\"vX\"}
  Rationale: restore known-good inference performance while investigating model-specific changes.

- Cause: GPU node instability (OOMs/driver errors)
  Immediate action: cordon and drain affected nodes, spin up replacement nodes in hetero-warm-pool, and reduce weight of pool in gpu-scheduler to zero for draining nodes.
  Commands: kubectl cordon <node>; kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data
  Rationale: remove noisy hardware from serving and restore capacity with healthy nodes.

- Cause: Network degradation (inter-region or overlay)
  Immediate action: switch affected routes to local-region-only routing policy, enable fallback to cached small models, and open ticket with cloud-networking for BGP/peering investigation.

Step-by-step mitigation (recommended order):
1) If batcher backlog: increase batcher replicas by +30% for affected region and lower max_batch_size in flight. Monitor queue length.
2) If cache drop: adjust kvcache TTL and rehydrate prefixes with warmup job (run warmup-runner --route __ROUTE__ --seed-keys 200).
3) If model suspect: trigger immediate rollback to previous model variant (see rollback API above) and mark rollout as halted in model-onboarding.
4) If GPU node problem: cordon/drain affected nodes, attach replacement nodes using gpu-autoscaler template.
5) If network: re-route traffic, mark incident with cloud-networking, and apply temporary geo-blocking for noisy paths.

Operational commands and snippets (copy/paste safe examples):
- Check recent deployments (Buildkite): bk list-builds --pipeline inference-deploy --branch main --limit 10
- Rollback model via curl:
  curl -X POST -H \"Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN\" -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" https://controlplane.redux.company/api/v1/model-router/rollbacks -d '{\"route\":\"chat-standard\",\"to_version\":\"v3.2.1\"}'
- Promote kvcache TTL via admin API:
  curl -X PATCH https://controlplane.redux.company/api/v1/kvcache/config -d '{\"route\":\"chat-standard\",\"ttl_seconds\":120}'
- Scale batcher deployment (k8s):
  kubectl -n serving scale deploy batcher-chat-standard --replicas=12

Escalation and communications:
- Triage update cadence: post initial summary in #infra-oncall within 10 minutes, then updates every 15 minutes until stabilized.
- If not resolved within 30 minutes: page on-call ENG-SRE lead, and open an incident in the incident tracker (use /incidents create).
- External customer impact: notify customer-success if more than two enterprise customers are reporting increased latency or SLA breaches.

Post-incident actions (within 72 hours):
1) Assemble a short RCA document and timeline in the incident tracker. Include relevant graphs: p95/p99 before, during, after; batcher queue length; kvcache hit rate; GPU node events.
2) Run a regression test matrix: replay representative workloads against a staging cluster with the suspect model or build.
3) If root cause is infra: raise an ADR or IaC change proposal (terraform change) to harden capacity, e.g., adjust node warm-pool sizing or add admission-controller guardrails.
4) Update runbook: add new detection rules or automated mitigations discovered during the incident.
5) Follow up action owner: assign remediation story to eng-infra backlog with priority P1/P2.

Testing this runbook (runbook game-day):
- Quarterly runbook drills: simulate a batcher backlog scenario by injecting synthetic traffic with high tokens-per-request and measure detection-to-mitigation time.
- Validation checklist: ensure rollback endpoints and admin APIs accept test tokens, confirm safe dry-run of cordon/drain commands, and verify Buildkite pipeline audit logs are available.

Appendix A — Alert table (config snippet):
| Alert ID | PromQL snippet | Trigger | Owner | Runbook action |
| LAT-ALERT-01 | histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(inference_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le,route)) > SLO | p95 breach 5m | eng-sre | follow triage checklist |
| LAT-ALERT-02 | sum(batcher_queue_length) > 50 | queue > 50 for 3m | serving-runtime | scale batcher / reduce max_batch |
| LAT-ALERT-03 | increase(kube_pod_container_status_last_termination_reason{reason=\"OOMKilled\"}[5m]) > 3 | >3 OOMs in 5m | infra-node-ops | cordon/drain nodes |

Appendix B — Owners and contacts:
- oncall Slack: #infra-oncall (primary channel)
- SRE lead pager: eng-sre-lead@oncall.pagerduty.com
- Serving runtime owner: @diego.alvarez (Diego Alvarez)
- Model onboarding: @samantha.green (Samantha Green)

Appendix C — Related runbooks and pages:
- gpu-hotspot-detection-and-preemptive-backfill-strategy (placement & capacity)
- kv-cache-reconstruction-and-eviction-resilience-runbook
- inference-network-aware-batching-and-routing-iac-design

Document maintenance: runbook owner (eng-infra) should review after every major GPU fleet change or model-serving rollout.

- 2024-11-05 created by Priya Menon
- 2025-02-12 updated: added PromQL snippets and expanded escalation cadence

title: GPU CI Dependency Graph and Promote Safety Net
preview:
Summary

This document defines the GPU CI dependency graph, the artifact promotion pipeline used to move GPU images and runtime bundles from CI -> QA -> Staging -> Production, and the safety net (telemetry, automatic rollback, manual gates) we use to prevent costly regressions. It is intended for infra engineers, SREs, and release engineers running GPU workloads and maintaining image/driver compatibility.

Goals

- Make promotion deterministic and auditable: every promoted artifact must show test/telemetry evidence and a signed provenance record.
- Minimize production regressions introduced by GPU runtime, kernel, or driver changes.
- Provide an automated safety net that can (a) prevent promotion, (b) enable safe canaries, and (c) rollback quickly if regressions are detected.

Scope

Applies to: GPU images, runtime bundles, kernel-module artifacts, operator charts, and IaC modules that directly affect GPU scheduling or networking. Not in scope: model checkpoints, evaluation-only artifacts.

Architecture overview (text diagram)

CI (pre-merge) -> Build/Packaging -> Artifact Signing -> QA Cluster (gpu-e2e) -> Promote Orchestrator -> Staging Canary -> Canary Telemetry Collector -> Production Rollout

Components

- ci-gpu-runner: GitHub Actions/self-hosted runners that execute GPU integration tests.
- gpu-builder: build job that runs container build, multi-arch packaging, and outputs layered artifacts to artifact-bucket.
- artifact-bucket: S3/GCS bucket with object-lock lifecycle for provenance.
- signer-keyring: cosign/keys managed in KMS used to sign artifacts and provenance metadata.
- promotion-orchestrator: internal service (k8s Job + controller) that applies promotion policies and records audits.
- telemetry-collector: Prometheus + Loki collection for canary runs and gpu-run metrics (nvml, kernel oops, host-cpu, memory).
- warm-pool-priming: warm GPU VMs/containers to stabilize first-request latency metrics.

Dependency graph generation

We generate a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of artifact dependencies at build time. Each node includes: artifact_id, base_image_sha, driver_version, kernel_compat_matrix (hash of supported kernels), build_tags, and signed_provenance_uri. The DAG is produced by the gpu-builder and committed as metadata alongside artifacts:

Example metadata snippet (JSON, stored as object "<artifact>.meta.json"):

{
  "artifact_id": "gpu-runtime-2025-02-12-abc123",
  "base_image_sha": "sha256:...",
  "driver_version": "540.XX",
  "kernel_compat_matrix": "kcm:v1:linux-5.4,linux-5.10,linux-6.1",
  "build_tags": ["nightly","cuda-12"],
  "signed_provenance_uri": "s3://artifact-bucket/provenance/gpu-runtime-2025-02-12.sig"
}

Promotion gates and pass criteria (text table)

Gate | Purpose | Owner | Pass criteria
-----|---------|-------|----------
Unit tests | Verify build integrity | ci-team | 100% required, flaky tests documented (<=1 flake/week)
Container CVE scan | Prevent known vuln regression | security | No critical or high CVEs with active exploits
Driver smoke | Ensure driver loads and NVML enumerates devices | eng-infra | NVML device_count == expected, no OOMs in 5 runs
E2E throughput | Validate throughput on a small workload | gpu-qe | >= 90% of baseline throughput (within window)
Warm-start latency | Ensure first-token latency for runtime path | product-slo | p95 <= 250ms for main workload
Telemetry sanity | No kernel OOPS, no memory leak signatures | SRE | Zero kernel oops, stable resident memory for 30m
Sign-off | Manual sign-off for major changes | release-owner | Release owner acknowledges changes and checks notes

Promotion workflow (step-by-step)

1) Pre-merge CI: Pull request must include build matrix metadata. Commands run:
   - ./ci/run-gpu-unit.sh --profile=presubmit
   - ./ci/build-and-publish.sh --bucket artifact-bucket --tag $PR_SHA

2) Artifact signing and DAG publish:
   - cosign sign --key kms://projects/redwood/keys/signer-keyring 
       s3://artifact-bucket/gpu-runtime:$PR_SHA
   - aws s3 cp <artifact>.meta.json s3://artifact-bucket/meta/$ARTIFACT_ID.json --acl bucket-owner-full-control

3) QA cluster verification (gpu-e2e):
   - promotion-orchestrator will schedule a qa-run job with the artifact.
   - telemetry-collector collects GPU metrics with labels: job=qa-run, artifact=$ARTIFACT_ID
   - if any gate fails, orchestrator returns promotion_failed and posts to #gpu-ci-alerts with artifact link.

4) Staging canary: automated canary of 5% of traffic with accelerated telemetry retention. Canary runs for a minimum of 2 hours and must meet the telemetry SLOs.

5) Production rollout: phased rollout (5%, 25%, 100%) with automatic pause on SLO breach.

Telemetry and SLOs (metric names and thresholds)

- gpu_nvml_device_count{job="qa-run"} == expected_device_count (no drift)
- gpu_host_memory_rss_bytes{service="runtime"}  (stable, no >10% growth over 30m)
- gpu_first_infer_latency_ms{pctl="p95"} < 250
- kernel_oops_count{host=~".*"} == 0
- node_restarts_total{job="kubelet"} increase > 0 triggers P0

Alerting and dashboards

- Dashboards: ci/gpu/promotion-overview, ci/gpu/canary-health, infra/gpu-driver-compatibility.
- Alerts:
  - ALERT:GPU_CANOARY_LATENCY (p95 > 250ms for 10m) -> Pager to oncall infra (severity: P1)
  - ALERT:GPU_KERNEL_OOPS (any increase) -> Pager P0 and auto-rollback trigger
  - ALERT:ARTIFACT_SIGN_MISSING (un-signed artifact) -> Slack #release-ops (warning)

Safety net behavior (automatic actions)

- If a P0 kernel oops is detected during staging or production canary, promotion-orchestrator triggers an immediate rollback to last_good_artifact and pauses the pipeline.
- For latency regressions that meet threshold (p95 > 250ms for 10m), the orchestrator pauses rollout, scales down canary to 0 traffic, and pages SRE.
- Any security-critical CVE discovered after promotion triggers immediate freeze of promotion queue and a required patch release.

Rollback playbook (quick steps)

1) Identify last_good_artifact via promotion-orchestrator UI or:
   - curl -s "$PROMO_API/latest_good?service=runtime" | jq .artifact_id
2) Trigger rollback:
   - ./promotionctl rollback --artifact $LAST_GOOD --env production --reason "kernel-oops-detected"
3) Confirm node health: check kernel_oops_count and node_restarts_total; run nvml smoke: ./tools/nvml-smoke.sh
4) Run a canary re-deploy with warm-pool priming for 30m; monitor dashboards.
5) Postmortem: open incident in PagerDuty + link to release and attach trace logs.

Canary tuning and warm pools

- Warm-pool-priming must ensure one container per target GPU is pre-initialized before canary traffic. Warm pools reduce first-request jitter and make telemetry signals less noisy.
- Warm pool size default: min(2, anticipated_canary_replicas). Increase for heavy model init (>2s).

Owner and responsibilities

- Build & Packaging: eng-infra/ci (owner: Marcus Patel)
- Promotion Orchestrator & Runbooks: eng-infra/release (owner: Leah Singh)
- Telemetry & Dashboards: SRE (owner: Diego Morales)
- Security gating & signing: security team (owner: Nina Park)

Operational runbook: when promotion fails

1) Read the failure reason from promotion-orchestrator UI and the artifact meta.
2) If gate == "Driver smoke" or "Kernel oops": escalate to oncall infra, capture host dmesg and nvml logs, and run rollback sequence.
3) If gate == "E2E throughput": compare to baseline in ci/gpu/perf-benchmarks and rerun with perf-profile=staging to rule out noise.
4) If gate == "Sign-off missing": require manual sign-off by release-owner before re-queue.
5) After mitigation, mark artifact as quarantined in artifact-bucket and tag with quarantine:<reason>.

Checklist for change authors (pre-promotion)

- [ ] Add compatibility matrix to PR artifact metadata.
- [ ] Ensure cosign signature is present and KMS key used is recorded.
- [ ] Run gpu-e2e locally using docker-compose / kube-kind with same driver image.
- [ ] Add release notes entry describing driver/kernel changes and risk.

Automation and future improvements (roadmap)

- Add ML-backed anomaly detection for subtle perf regressions using historical canary traces.
- Automate kernel-compat verification across a matrix of cloud images using a scheduled batch job.
- Integrate SBOM pipeline to ensure transitive dependency visibility for GPU drivers.

Appendix: sample commands and snippets

Sign artifact with cosign (example):

cosign sign --key kms://projects/redwood/locations/global/keyRings/releases/cryptoKeys/signer-keyring \
  s3://artifact-bucket/gpu-runtime:$ARTIFACT_TAG

Promotion CLI example:

promotionctl promote --artifact $ARTIFACT_ID --from qa --to staging --policy=phased --notify=#gpu-ci-alerts

Warm pool priming example (kubectl template):

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: warm-pool-primer
spec:
  replicas: 2
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: primer
        image: s3://artifact-bucket/gpu-runtime:$ARTIFACT_ID
        command: ["/bin/sh","-c","/opt/primer/warmup.sh"]
EOF

Contact and escalation

- Primary oncall: eng-infra oncall rotation (PagerDuty)
- Slack channel: #gpu-ci-alerts (automated messages and actionable links)
- Pager escalation: P0->sre lead, P1->eng-infra oncall

Revision history

- 2024-11-05: Created by Ava Chen.
- 2024-12-12: Added auto-rollback criteria and telemetry metric names (reviewers: Marcus Patel).
- 2025-02-12: Finalized canary window and warm-pool guidance (reviewers: Leah Singh, Diego Morales).

- [ ] Run gpu-e2e locally using docker-compose / kube-kind with same driver image.
- [ ] Add release notes entry describing driver/kernel changes and risk.

Automation and future improvements (roadmap)

- Add ML-backed anomaly detection for subtle perf regressions using historical canary traces.
- Automate kernel-compat verification across a matrix of cloud images using a scheduled batch job.
- Integrate SBOM pipeline to ensure transitive dependency visibility for GPU drivers.

Appendix: sample commands and snippets

Sign artifact with cosign (example):

cosign sign --key kms://projects/redwood/locations/global/keyRings/releases/cryptoKeys/signer-keyring \
  s3://artifact-bucket/gpu-runtime:$ARTIFACT_TAG

Promotion CLI example:

promotionctl promote --artifact $ARTIFACT_ID --from qa --to staging --policy=phased --notify=#gpu-ci-alerts

Warm pool priming example (kubectl template):

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: warm-pool-primer
spec:
  replicas: 2
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: primer
        image: s3://artifact-bucket/gpu-runtime:$ARTIFACT_ID
        command: ["/bin/sh","-c","/opt/primer/warmup.sh"]
EOF

Contact and escalation

- Primary oncall: eng-infra oncall rotation (PagerDuty)
- Slack channel: #gpu-ci-alerts (automated messages and actionable links)
- Pager escalation: P0->sre lead, P1->eng-infra oncall

Revision history

- 2024-11-05: Created by Ava Chen.
- 2024-12-12: Added auto-rollback criteria and telemetry metric names (reviewers: Marcus Patel).
- 2025-02-12: Finalized canary window and warm-pool guidance (reviewers: Leah Singh, Diego Morales).

title: GPU Eviction Smoothing & Credit Scheduler — Spec and Runbook
preview: This document specifies a tenant-aware eviction smoothing and credit-based scheduler designed to reduce noisy preemption impact, improve tail latency for interactive inference, and provide a predictable tenant billing signal tied to eviction exposure. It combines a short-horizon predictive filter, time-decayed credit buckets, and topology-aware placement to hedge against large simultaneous preemptions and enable graceful degradation to burst/backfill pools.

It combines a short-horizon predictive filter, time-decayed credit buckets, and topology-aware placement to hedge against large simultaneous preemptions and enable graceful degradation to burst/backfill pools.

title: GPU Capacity Runway and Auto‑Sinking Playbook
preview:
Summary

This playbook defines the GPU capacity runway model, the automated "auto-sinking" policy to proactively downscale warm/standby pools when project spending runway is threatened, and the required Terraform/CI gating, monitoring, and rollback controls. It is intended for infra owners, SRE oncall, and finance partners.

Motivation

- Reduce idle GPU costs without violating tenant SLOs.
- Provide deterministic, audit-able actions that map capacity decisions to financial runway (days-of-runway).
- Ensure safety: changes must pass IaC and smoke tests, be canaryed, and have an immediate rollback path.

Definitions

- Runway (days): projected days before committed budget plus forecasted revenue cannot cover expected spend at current utilization.
- Warm pool: pre-provisioned GPU instances (or pods) kept ready for fast start (<90s).
- Auto‑sinking: automated reduction of warm pool target sizes driven by runway thresholds.
- Conservative mode: limits burst and enforces strict preemption/fallback policies.

Key services and components

- gpu-allocator-svc (internal): matches requests to pools; exposes metrics: warm_pool_fill_time_seconds, unused_gpu_minutes_total.
- capacity-sentry (cron service): computes runway daily and emits runway_days gauge.
- burst-orchestrator: coordinates cross-region burst when warm pools are reduced.
- billing-bridge: reconciles infra usage to finance forecasts (exported daily CSV to s3://billing-bridge/runway/).

Metrics and sources

Primary signals used to compute runway: 
1) Committed budget remaining (finance API): finance.commit_remaining_usd
2) Recent spend rate (30d trailing average): billing-bridge.spend_usd_30d_avg
3) Effective utilization: sum(request_token_seconds)/sum(allocated_gpu_seconds) from gpu-telemetry
4) Logistic safety buffer: defaults to 3 days to account for forecast error

Runway calculation (simplified):
runway_days = (commit_remaining_usd) / (spend_usd_30d_avg / 30) - logistic_buffer_days

Policy thresholds and actions (table)

Threshold | Action | Safety constraints
---|---|---
> 60 days | No action | Maintain default warm pool targets
30-60 days | Reduced warm pool by 10% | CI gated change, non-disruptive deallocation
14-30 days | Reduce warm pool by 25% & enable conservative mode | Can trigger burst fallback if warm pool drains
<14 days | Auto-sink aggressive: reduce warm pool 50% + pause non-critical eval jobs | Immediate paging to SRE and Finance; canary rollback enabled

SLOs and constraints

- Warm pool fill time target: 95th percentile <= 90s.
- Provisioning failure rate must remain <= 1% per hour.
- Tenant availability SLO: 99.9% for hosted API tier; any policy must not drop this below 99.7% for more than 15 minutes.

Runbook: sequence to enable Auto‑Sinking (operator steps)

1) Review automated runway report (capacity-sentry) in #infra-runway Slack channel.
2) Confirm finance.commit_remaining_usd and billing-bridge.spend_usd_30d_avg match expectations. If mismatch, escalate to Finance.
3) If runway_days < configured threshold, open an incident with tag type: cost-runway and set priority = P2.
4) Trigger terraform plan via infra pipeline (see CI section) with variable auto_sink_proposal = true to preview changes.
5) Run integration smoke tests against canary region (see CI test matrix).
6) If smoke tests pass, merge plan into canary workspace (region=europe-west1) with roll_percent=10.
7) Monitor for 30 minutes; if metrics (warm_pool_fill_time_seconds, provisioning_failure_rate) remain within SLO, continue rollout to additional regions (increment by 25% every 30 minutes).
8) If any SLO breach occurs, immediately run terraform apply with revert_auto_sink = true in affected workspace and notify SRE/owner.

Terraform / IaC requirements

- All changes must be implemented via the module: modules/gpu_warmpool (registry version pinned in tfworkspaces).
- Required input variables added: auto_sink_enabled (bool), auto_sink_policy (string), runway_threshold_days (number).
- A sample HCL snippet (to be included in PRs):

```hcl
module "gpu_warmpool" {
  source = "git::ssh://git@repo.internal/infra/modules//gpu_warmpool?ref=v2025.02"

  region                   = var.region
  target_size              = var.target_size
  auto_sink_enabled        = true
  auto_sink_policy         = "runway-v2"
  runway_threshold_days    = 30
}
```

- State separation: use dedicated Terraform workspace per region. Plan outputs must include a cost_delta_estimate (see module output). Any plan with cost_delta_estimate > 3000 USD requires a finance approver.

CI/CD gating and tests

Pipeline: infra/gpu-auto-sink (Buildkite) steps:
- step: terragrunt:terraform-plan (variables: auto_sink_proposal=true)
- step: terratest:unit (fast)
- step: post-plan-smoke (kitchen-style) -> spins up 2 ephemeral gpus in canary workspace and runs 10 synthetic inference requests
- step: cost-approval-check (if cost_delta_estimate > 3000 USD)
- step: manual-approval (for initial rollout) -> for subsequent automated rollouts, use automatic gating after 7 successful canaries

Required tests for PRs touching modules/gpu_warmpool:
- Unit: hcl-lint and module contract tests (must cover outputs: cost_delta_estimate, expected_rollback_plan).
- Integration: terratest (runs in sandbox account) verifying scaling up/down and idempotency.
- Chaos test (nightly): simulate provisioning failure and ensure automatic rollback retains at least 50% of previous warm pool capacity.

Alerting and dashboards

Alerts (Prometheus style rules):
- ALERT: RunwayCritical
  IF capacity_sentry_runway_days < 14
  FOR 10m
  LABELS: severity=critical, team=infra
  ANNOTATIONS: summary = "GPU runway below 14 days"

- ALERT: WarmPoolFillLatencyHigh
  IF gpu_allocator_warm_pool_fill_time_seconds{quantile="0.95"} > 90
  FOR 5m
  LABELS: severity=high

Dashboards: "Runway Overview" (grafana) showing: runway_days, spend_30d_avg, warm_pool_target_vs_actual, provisioning_failure_rate, cost_delta_estimate.

Emergency rollback procedure

1) From the incident runbook page, click the quicklink to Canary workspace (link: https://terraform.console/workspaces/gpu-canary-europe-west1).
2) Run `terraform apply -var='revert_auto_sink=true'` and confirm the apply. Sample CLI: `TFC_WORKSPACE=gpu-canary-europe-west1 terragrunt apply --auto-approve -var='revert_auto_sink=true'`.
3) If Terraform fails, use the allocator API to set warm_pool_target via: `curl -X POST https://internal-api/gpu-allocator/v1/warmpool/target -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -d '{"region":"europe-west1","target": 120}'`.
4) Notify #oncall-infra and #finance with incident id and attach plan logs.

Ownership and escalation

- Primary owner: infra-capacity (team alias). Responsible for module maintenance, alert tuning, and CI pipelines.
- Secondary owner: cost-ops (Finance/SRE cross-functional). Responsible for runway model accuracy and approving high-impact changes.
- Escalation matrix: owner -> infra-eng lead -> SRE director -> CFO (if runway falls < 7 days).

Rollout plan and guardrails

- Week 0: Dry-run in sandbox account; no auto-sinking, only alerts.
- Week 1: Enable auto-sink in canary region with manual-approval gate.
- Week 2-3: Gradual rollout to 3 production regions at 10%-35%-100% with 30-minute observation windows.
- Guardrails: automatic interrupts if any region's tenant availability SLO dips below 99.8% for 10 minutes.

Observability and audit trail

- Every automated action emits an audit event to audit-log-cluster with fields: actor=capacity-sentry, policy=runway-v2, before_targets, after_targets, runway_days, commit_id.
- Daily runway report stored at s3://infra-runway/reports/YYYY-MM-DD.json and surfaced to Finance via scheduled BigQuery ingestion.

Appendix A: Example Prometheus alert rule

```yaml
groups:
- name: capacity.rules
  rules:
  - alert: RunwayCritical
    expr: capacity_sentry_runway_days < 14
    for: 10m
    labels:
      severity: critical
      team: infra
    annotations:
      summary: "GPU runway below 14 days"
```

Appendix B: Minimal terraform plan checker (bash)

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
plan_json="$1"
cost_delta=$(jq '.cost_delta_estimate' < "$plan_json")
if (( $(echo "$cost_delta > 3000" | bc -l) )); then
  echo "Cost delta > 3000 USD -> requires finance approval"
  exit 2
fi
echo "Plan within threshold"
```

Notes and future work

- Replace static logistic buffer with ML-backed forecast error model (Q2 2025).
- Integrate warm-pool scheduling with heterogenous GPU topology (MIG-aware placements).

Related documents

- See related pages list for forecasting and provisioning DR docs.

Contact

- For questions: elena.marquez@redwood.com, @elena on Slack.

title: GPU experimentation sandbox — governance, quota rules, and operator playbook
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This page defines governance, quota rules, and the operator playbook for Redwood Inference's GPU Experimentation Sandbox (the ‘Sandbox’). The Sandbox is a self-service environment for short-term experiments, model profiling, microbenchmarks, and pre-production evals. It balances developer velocity with cost control, safety, and fair-share across teams.

Scope
- Environment: ephemeral Sandbox clusters (non-production, tagged `sandbox/*`).
- Workloads: jobs that are exploratory, short-duration (<= 48h default) or flagged as non-customer-facing.
- Not allowed: production inference endpoints, regulated data processing, and customer PII workloads. See Security policy for exceptions.

Goals
1. Provide low-latency self-service GPU access for developers and researchers.
2. Ensure predictable cost and prevent noisy-neighbor impact on production pools.
3. Enforce per-team quotas, preemption policies, and GitOps validation to reduce drift.
4. Provide auditing, telemetry, and simple remediation playbooks for oncall.

Roles & Responsibilities
- Sandbox Owner: eng-infra (Priya Natarajan) — maintains operator manifests, quota defaults, dashboards.
- Team Owners: individual product/ML teams — request quota increases, manage experiments.
- Cost & Billing: finance — receives monthly quota burn reports.
- Security: security team — approves any exceptions for restricted data.
- Oncall: infra-sre — first responder for eviction incidents impacting production isolation.

Quota model (summary)
- Per-team monthly quota (GPU-hours): default 200 GPU-hours/month for Developer tier.
- Per-project ephemeral lease limit: max 4 GPUs, 48h per lease; auto-evict after expiry.
- Priority buckets: 
  - dev (default, preemptible)
  - research (higher burst, limited quota, preemptible)
  - infra-exempt (reserved capacity for infra team only)
- Soft vs hard limits: soft alerts at 80% usage; hard enforcement at 100% (leases denied).

Quota enforcement and automation
- Implementation components:
  - GitOps manifests for sandbox namespaces (repo: infra/sandbox-gitops).
  - Terraform module `tf-sandbox-tenant` (applies IAM, namespace, cost-centre tag, quota CRDs).
  - Kubernetes operator `sandbox-allocator` (custom controller) that a) validates leases, b) enforces runtime limits, c) issues eviction when expiry/hard-limit reached.
  - OPA/Gatekeeper policy for validating sandbox manifests before promotion (checks labels, resource requests, and image provenance).

Table: Key enforcement knobs
| Knob | Default | Enforcement mechanism | Owner | Notes |
|------|---------|-----------------------|-------|-------|
| Monthly GPU-hours per team | 200 | Quota CRD + operator enforcement | eng-infra | Adjustable via quota-request flow |
| Max GPUs per lease | 4 | Operator checks at create time | eng-infra | Hard limit to avoid cluster hogging |
| Lease duration | 48h | Operator TTL + controller eviction | eng-infra | Auto-evict and snapshot logs |
| Preemption class | preemptible | Scheduler taints + priority | eng-infra | Can be bumped by infra-exempt bucket |

Onboarding and change flow (numbered steps)
1. Team creates a sandbox tenant PR in infra/sandbox-gitops with `tenant.yaml` (example below).
2. OPA/Gatekeeper policy runs on PR; CI returns fast feedback (blocking).
3. Once policy checks pass, a reviewer from eng-infra approves and merges.
4. Terraform pipeline (concourse/buildkite) picks up change, runs `tf-sandbox-tenant` plan and apply.
5. `sandbox-allocator` reconcile loop observes new Quota CRD and creates default lease-bucket.
6. Team can issue `kubectl sandbox lease create` or use the UI.

Example tenant.yaml (single-file example used in GitOps repo)
```
apiVersion: sandbox.redwood.io/v1
kind: Tenant
metadata:
  name: ai-platform-research
  labels:
    cost-center: ai-platform
spec:
  defaultQuotaGpuHours: 200
  maxGpusPerLease: 4
  defaultLeaseDuration: 48h
  priorityBucket: research
```

CI/CD and GitOps validation (technical details)
- CI pipeline: buildkite pipeline `infra/sandbox-gitops` performs:
  1) static manifest lint (kubeval + yamllint)
  2) OPA policy checks (rego: sandbox/policies/*)
  3) terratest acceptance for `tf-sandbox-tenant` (light-weight)
  4) cost-estimate smoke test (estimates GPU-hours vs quota)
- Promotion gate: merge is blocked if policies fail. A special `infra:fast-track` label can be used for urgent infra fixes (requires two infra approvers).

Observability and dashboards
- Dashboards (Grafana):
  - Sandbox Overview: per-team GPU-hours burn, active leases, avg lease duration.
  - Noisy Neighbor Monitor: cross-pool latency spike correlation (logs from gpu-metrics-agent).
  - Eviction & TTL Dashboard: eviction reasons, counts, top offending leases.
- Alerts (PagerDuty):
  - P1: unexpected eviction of >25% leases in a region within 30m (oncall).
  - P2: team exceeds 120% of monthly quota forecast (email+slack to team owner).

Cost reporting and chargeback
- Daily export: sandbox-cost-exporter posts per-team GPU-hour usage into billing S3 (CSV) at 06:00 UTC.
- Monthly reconciliation: finance-runbook consumes export and applies internal chargebacks or quota top-ups.

Eviction & remediation playbook (short)
1. Operator auto-evicts lease on TTL expiry; lease owner receives email + Slack DM with logs and checkpoint link.
2. If eviction is due to hard quota enforcement (100% cap), team must open a quota-request ticket via internal form (link in email).
3. If eviction cascades to production isolation concerns (Noisy Neighbor rule), infra-sre triages immediately:
   a. escalate to oncall primary, gather pod logs and GPU metrics (commands below).
   b. isolate offending namespace (taint + cordon pattern) and migrate critical workloads to warm-pool.

Commands & snippets
- Create a temporary lease (CLI):
  - kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: sandbox.redwood.io/v1
kind: Lease
metadata:
  name: quick-bench-2025-02
spec:
  tenant: ai-platform-research
  gpus: 1
  duration: 6h
EOF
- Operator debug (oncall):
  - kubectl get leases --all-namespaces -o wide --label=priorityBucket=research
  - kubectl logs -n infra $(kubectl get pods -n infra -l app=sandbox-allocator -o name) --since=2h
  - /opt/reports/gpu-checker --since 30m --metrics-endpoint https://gpu-metrics.internal:9090

OPA policy excerpt (human readable)
- Disallow images from `:latest` tag.
- Require `cost-center` label with valid org-code.
- Deny deployments with `requests.cpu` > 16 or `nvidia.com/gpu` > 8 in a single pod for dev priority.

Terraform module configuration (reference table)
| Variable | Type | Default | Description |
|----------|------|---------|-------------|
| tenant_name | string | n/a | name of tenant namespace |
| default_quota_gpu_hours | int | 200 | default monthly GPU-hours |
| max_gpus_per_lease | int | 4 | hard cap per lease |
| owner_email | string | n/a | primary contact for quota alerts |

Operational run scenarios (decision matrix)
- Scenario: bursty research jobs causing wider cluster pressure
  - Action: enable temporary burst-limit (infra-sre) -> reduce soft limits for non-essential tenants -> notify teams -> schedule warm-pool for critical jobs.
- Scenario: team requests permanent quota increase > 500 GPU-hours
  - Action: product/finance review -> risk assessment -> approve/deny -> if approved, update GitOps tenant manifest and run plan.

Audit & security considerations
- All sandbox API calls and `kubectl` actions are audited via cluster-audit-log and forwarded to security S3 for 180-day retention.
- Any tenant requesting exceptions for regulated data must open a security SR and receive written approval before creating leases.

Appendix: operational links and contact points
- GitOps repo: git+ssh://git.redwood.internal/infra/sandbox-gitops.git
- Terraform module: git+ssh://git.redwood.internal/infra/terraform-modules//tf-sandbox-tenant
- Operator service name: `infra/sandbox-allocator` (K8s namespace `infra`)
- Oncall rota: infra-sre (PagerDuty schedule: RI_INFRASRE)
- Quota request form: https://internal.redwood.com/forms/sandbox-quota-request

Change history
- 2024-07-12: created (initial draft).
- 2024-09-05: added OPA policy checklist and CI gating details.
- 2025-02-03: published: updated quota defaults and new eviction alert thresholds.

If you are an owner of a team that requires an exception or higher quota, follow the onboarding flow above and tag `@eng-infra` in your PR. For urgent questions, ping #infra-sre on Slack.
text:
Overview

Purpose: This page defines governance, quota rules, and the operator playbook for Redwood Inference's GPU Experimentation Sandbox (the ‘Sandbox’). The Sandbox is a self-service environment for short-term experiments, model profiling, microbenchmarks, and pre-production evals. It balances developer velocity with cost control, safety, and fair-share across teams.

Scope
- Environment: ephemeral Sandbox clusters (non-production, tagged `sandbox/*`).
- Workloads: jobs that are exploratory, short-duration (<= 48h default) or flagged as non-customer-facing.
- Not allowed: production inference endpoints, regulated data processing, and customer PII workloads. See Security policy for exceptions.

Goals
1. Provide low-latency self-service GPU access for developers and researchers.
2. Ensure predictable cost and prevent noisy-neighbor impact on production pools.
3. Enforce per-team quotas, preemption policies, and GitOps validation to reduce drift.
4. Provide auditing, telemetry, and simple remediation playbooks for oncall.

Roles & Responsibilities
- Sandbox Owner: eng-infra (Priya Natarajan) — maintains operator manifests, quota defaults, dashboards.
- Team Owners: individual product/ML teams — request quota increases, manage experiments.
- Cost & Billing: finance — receives monthly quota burn reports.
- Security: security team — approves any exceptions for restricted data.
- Oncall: infra-sre — first responder for eviction incidents impacting production isolation.

Quota model (summary)
- Per-team monthly quota (GPU-hours): default 200 GPU-hours/month for Developer tier.
- Per-project ephemeral lease limit: max 4 GPUs, 48h per lease; auto-evict after expiry.
- Priority buckets: 
  - dev (default, preemptible)
  - research (higher burst, limited quota, preemptible)
  - infra-exempt (reserved capacity for infra team only)
- Soft vs hard limits: soft alerts at 80% usage; hard enforcement at 100% (leases denied).

Quota enforcement and automation
- Implementation components:
  - GitOps manifests for sandbox namespaces (repo: infra/sandbox-gitops).
  - Terraform module `tf-sandbox-tenant` (applies IAM, namespace, cost-centre tag, quota CRDs).
  - Kubernetes operator `sandbox-allocator` (custom controller) that a) validates leases, b) enforces runtime limits, c) issues eviction when expiry/hard-limit reached.
  - OPA/Gatekeeper policy for validating sandbox manifests before promotion (checks labels, resource requests, and image provenance).

Table: Key enforcement knobs
| Knob | Default | Enforcement mechanism | Owner | Notes |
|------|---------|-----------------------|-------|-------|
| Monthly GPU-hours per team | 200 | Quota CRD + operator enforcement | eng-infra | Adjustable via quota-request flow |
| Max GPUs per lease | 4 | Operator checks at create time | eng-infra | Hard limit to avoid cluster hogging |
| Lease duration | 48h | Operator TTL + controller eviction | eng-infra | Auto-evict and snapshot logs |
| Preemption class | preemptible | Scheduler taints + priority | eng-infra | Can be bumped by infra-exempt bucket |

Onboarding and change flow (numbered steps)
1. Team creates a sandbox tenant PR in infra/sandbox-gitops with `tenant.yaml` (example below).
2. OPA/Gatekeeper policy runs on PR; CI returns fast feedback (blocking).
3. Once policy checks pass, a reviewer from eng-infra approves and merges.
4. Terraform pipeline (concourse/buildkite) picks up change, runs `tf-sandbox-tenant` plan and apply.
5. `sandbox-allocator` reconcile loop observes new Quota CRD and creates default lease-bucket.
6. Team can issue `kubectl sandbox lease create` or use the UI.

Example tenant.yaml (single-file example used in GitOps repo)
```
apiVersion: sandbox.redwood.io/v1
kind: Tenant
metadata:
  name: ai-platform-research
  labels:
    cost-center: ai-platform
spec:
  defaultQuotaGpuHours: 200
  maxGpusPerLease: 4
  defaultLeaseDuration: 48h
  priorityBucket: research
```

CI/CD and GitOps validation (technical details)
- CI pipeline: buildkite pipeline `infra/sandbox-gitops` performs:
  1) static manifest lint (kubeval + yamllint)
  2) OPA policy checks (rego: sandbox/policies/*)
  3) terratest acceptance for `tf-sandbox-tenant` (light-weight)
  4) cost-estimate smoke test (estimates GPU-hours vs quota)
- Promotion gate: merge is blocked if policies fail. A special `infra:fast-track` label can be used for urgent infra fixes (requires two infra approvers).

Observability and dashboards
- Dashboards (Grafana):
  - Sandbox Overview: per-team GPU-hours burn, active leases, avg lease duration.
  - Noisy Neighbor Monitor: cross-pool latency spike correlation (logs from gpu-metrics-agent).
  - Eviction & TTL Dashboard: eviction reasons, counts, top offending leases.
- Alerts (PagerDuty):
  - P1: unexpected eviction of >25% leases in a region within 30m (oncall).
  - P2: team exceeds 120% of monthly quota forecast (email+slack to team owner).

Cost reporting and chargeback
- Daily export: sandbox-cost-exporter posts per-team GPU-hour usage into billing S3 (CSV) at 06:00 UTC.
- Monthly reconciliation: finance-runbook consumes export and applies internal chargebacks or quota top-ups.

Eviction & remediation playbook (short)
1. Operator auto-evicts lease on TTL expiry; lease owner receives email + Slack DM with logs and checkpoint link.
2. If eviction is due to hard quota enforcement (100% cap), team must open a quota-request ticket via internal form (link in email).
3. If eviction cascades to production isolation concerns (Noisy Neighbor rule), infra-sre triages immediately:
   a. escalate to oncall primary, gather pod logs and GPU metrics (commands below).
   b. isolate offending namespace (taint + cordon pattern) and migrate critical workloads to warm-pool.

Commands & snippets
- Create a temporary lease (CLI):
  - kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: sandbox.redwood.io/v1
kind: Lease
metadata:
  name: quick-bench-2025-02
spec:
  tenant: ai-platform-research
  gpus: 1
  duration: 6h
EOF
- Operator debug (oncall):
  - kubectl get leases --all-namespaces -o wide --label=priorityBucket=research
  - kubectl logs -n infra $(kubectl get pods -n infra -l app=sandbox-allocator -o name) --since=2h
  - /opt/reports/gpu-checker --since 30m --metrics-endpoint https://gpu-metrics.internal:9090

OPA policy excerpt (human readable)
- Disallow images from `:latest` tag.
- Require `cost-center` label with valid org-code.
- Deny deployments with `requests.cpu` > 16 or `nvidia.com/gpu` > 8 in a single pod for dev priority.

Terraform module configuration (reference table)
| Variable | Type | Default | Description |
|----------|------|---------|-------------|
| tenant_name | string | n/a | name of tenant namespace |
| default_quota_gpu_hours | int | 200 | default monthly GPU-hours |
| max_gpus_per_lease | int | 4 | hard cap per lease |
| owner_email | string | n/a | primary contact for quota alerts |

Operational run scenarios (decision matrix)
- Scenario: bursty research jobs causing wider cluster pressure
  - Action: enable temporary burst-limit (infra-sre) -> reduce soft limits for non-essential tenants -> notify teams -> schedule warm-pool for critical jobs.
- Scenario: team requests permanent quota increase > 500 GPU-hours
  - Action: product/finance review -> risk assessment -> approve/deny -> if approved, update GitOps tenant manifest and run plan.

Audit & security considerations
- All sandbox API calls and `kubectl` actions are audited via cluster-audit-log and forwarded to security S3 for 180-day retention.
- Any tenant requesting exceptions for regulated data must open a security SR and receive written approval before creating leases.

Appendix: operational links and contact points
- GitOps repo: git+ssh://git.redwood.internal/infra/sandbox-gitops.git
- Terraform module: git+ssh://git.redwood.internal/infra/terraform-modules//tf-sandbox-tenant
- Operator service name: `infra/sandbox-allocator` (K8s namespace `infra`)
- Oncall rota: infra-sre (PagerDuty schedule: RI_INFRASRE)
- Quota request form: https://internal.redwood.com/forms/sandbox-quota-request

Change history
- 2024-07-12: created (initial draft).
- 2024-09-05: added OPA policy checklist and CI gating details.
- 2025-02-03: published: updated quota defaults and new eviction alert thresholds.

If you are an owner of a team that requires an exception or higher quota, follow the onboarding flow above and tag `@eng-infra` in your PR. For urgent questions, ping #infra-sre on Slack.

title: GPU offline capacity reclamation and tenant thaw protocol
preview:
Summary
This runbook describes the end-to-end process for reclaiming GPU capacity that has been declared offline (hardware fault, maintenance, prolonged preemption or lost heartbeats), and the controlled "thaw" procedure for returning tenants/workloads to that reclaimed capacity. The goal is to minimize customer impact, preserve SLOs, keep billing/credits consistent, and ensure Terraform/IaC and CI/CD artifacts remain the source of truth.

When to use this doc
- A node or GPU is marked OFFLINE by fleet telemetry for > 5 minutes and is not automatically rejoining.
- Eviction/maintenance window has completed but the node failed health checks.
- A region-level cold-power recovery or rack maintenance requires manual reclamation.

Owner and escalation
- Primary owner: GPU Fleet Capacity team (oncall: gpu-capacity@pagerduty)
- Secondary owner: Cloud Accounts & Networking (for VPC/egress issues)
- Escalation path: oncall -> eng-infra lead -> infra SRE -> CTO ops

SLO context and impact windows
- Tenant interactive SLO (p50 latency): 120ms target; we consider mitigation if expected 15-minute impact > 5% of tenant traffic.
- Throughput SLOs for reserved Dedicated capacity: maintain >= 96% of committed tps over rolling 1h window.
- Billing/credits: tenants on soft-reserve (warm pool credits) should not lose credit buckets during automated reconciliation; hard-reserve tenants may be moved with billing-notify.

Pre-flight checks (automated + manual)
1) Confirm telemetry signals: fleet-telemetry query for node_id shows offline_reason != "scheduled-drain" AND offline_duration > 300s.
2) Check KV-cache state: verify token prefix warm cache snapshot exists for affected tenant(s).
3) Validate network reachability: run egress and private-link checks to control plane.
4) Identify tenant impact: run the tenant-impact tool: ./bin/tenant-impact --node NODE_ID --window 15m

Immediate automated remediation (0–10 min)
- If offline_reason == "transient-preempt" and offline_duration < 600s: let autoscaler keep warm pool and do no-op.
- Otherwise trigger the reclaim pipeline: /ops/reclaim/run --node NODE_ID --mode dry-run and collect the output.

Manual reclamation and verification (10–45 min)
Step 1 — Coordinate:
- Announce to #infra-notifications with subject: "[GPU RECLAIM] NODE <NODE_ID> region:<REGION>" and tag affected tenant teams.
- Open a short incident channel if tenant impact > 5% or SLO risk exists.

Step 2 — Non-destructive snapshot and metadata freeze:
- Run: fleetctl snapshot create --node NODE_ID --reason "reclaim-manual-<YYYYMMDD>"
- Freeze billing/credit delta for tenants on this node: billingctl freeze --node NODE_ID --until 1h

Step 3 — Diagnostic probes (run in sequence):
1. hardware self-test: hwdiag run --node NODE_ID --run quick
2. kernel logs: journalctl -u gpu-driver -o short-iso --since "15 minutes ago" | sed -n '1,200p'
3. nvml state: /opt/tools/nvml-dump --node NODE_ID --json > /tmp/nvml-NODE_ID.json
4. network: socat test to control-plane and tenant endpoints (examples below)

Example commands and snippets:
- Check control plane reachability:
  curl -s -f https://control.redwood.internal/v1/health || echo "control plane unreachable"
- Validate service-level egress to tenant backend:
  ip netns exec infra-probe curl -sS http://10.10.0.22:8080/health || echo "tenant backend fail"

Decision matrix (reclaim vs escalate)
- If hardware test fails or NVML reports persistent ECC errors: escalate to hardware team and mark node for decommissioning.
- If network is flaky but node hardware OK: attempt soft-reconfigure (see Network remediation).
- If node can be rejoined after reboot and tests pass: perform controlled join and prepare tenant-thaw plan.

Network remediation (if applicable)
- Reapply VPC attachments: terraform apply -target=module.gpu_vpc_attachment --auto-approve
- Restart egress sidecar on node: kubectl -n infra rollout restart daemonset/egress-proxy --field-selector spec.nodeName=NODE_NAME
- If NAT gateway ip exhaustion is suspected, switch to alternate egress pool: egressctl swap-pool --node NODE_ID --pool alt-egress-pool

Reclaim action (if node will not return within 1h)
1) Mark node state: fleetctl node set --node NODE_ID --state RECLAIMED --owner gpu-capacity
2) Drain tenant allocations: allocatorctl drain --node NODE_ID --grace-period 120 --preserve-credits
3) Create IAM/service-account rebinds for affected tenant workloads (automated via terraform module change, example below)

Terraform/IaC pattern (maintain source of truth)
- We never do permanent capacity reassignments out-of-band. All changes must be represented in Terraform. The reclaim-controller writes a tfvars patch which must be reviewed and merged.
- Typical tfvars patch example (terraform variables file):
| variable | old_value | new_value | comment |
| gpu_node_pool.size | 24 | 23 | reclaim NODE_ID-xyz |

CLI: create patch bundle
  ./tools/tf-patch --pool gpu-node-pool/region-1 --dec -1 --reason "reclaim NODE_ID-xyz" --out tfpatch/reclaim-NODE_ID.patch

Merge process (manual safe path)
1. Create PR with tfpatch and automated plan artifact. CI will run plan validation including simulation of placement.
2. Wait for at least one reviewer from gpu-fleet and one from cloud-accounts.
3. Merge during a non-peak window unless SLO risk requires immediate merge (document exception in PR description).

Tenant thaw process (controlled reallocation)
- Preconditions: reclaimed node repaired and marked Joinable OR replacement node provisioned and tf plan applied. KV-caches warmed or snapshot restore available.

Thaw stages (per-tenant)
1) Thaw scheduling: allocate a small pilot percentage of traffic (5–10%) to reclaimed capacity for 15 minutes.
2) Monitor: use the tenant-thaw dashboard; key metrics: p50/p95 latency, error rate, KV cache hit ratio, token billing delta.
3) Ramp: if pilot stable, increase allocation in doubling increments: 10% -> 25% -> 50% -> 100% with 10–30 minute observation windows.
4) Full reattach: once at 100% for at least 30 minutes with no regressions, remove draining markers and unfreeze billing.

Tenant thaw CLI example:
  allocatorctl attach --tenant TENANT_ID --target-node NODE_ID --percentage 10 --pilot --preserve-credits
  # wait 15m, run monitors
  allocatorctl scale-increment --tenant TENANT_ID --node NODE_ID --factor 2

Safety and rollback criteria
- Abort and rollback thaw if:
  - p95 latency increases > 30% from baseline for two consecutive windows;
  - error rate increases from 0.2% to > 1%;
  - KV cache hit ratio drops > 15% and cannot be recovered by cache warmup within 20 minutes.
- Rollback steps: run allocatorctl detach --tenant TENANT_ID --node NODE_ID --immediate and route traffic back to previous pool. Create incident with tags: gpu-thaw-rollback.

Post-reclaim auditing and reconciliation (within 24h)
- Reconcile Terraform changes: ensure PRs merged and state applied. Run terraform state list and ensure no dangling references to NODE_ID.
- Financial reconciliation: billingctl unfreeze --node NODE_ID and run credits-audit --node NODE_ID --window 24h; file any adjustments with Finance (ticket in Jira: FIN-GPU-ADJ).
- Telemetry audit: run fleet-telemetry audit --node NODE_ID --since 48h and attach to incident.

CI/CD and runbook integration points
- All reclaim controller changes must have unit tests for simulated preemption and egress failures.
- CI job: gpu-reclaim-integration is required to pass before merging allocator controller or tf-patch tooling.
- Canary pattern: when reclaim logic is changed, deploy to blue sandbox with synthetic tenants using the warm-pool test harness.

Operational tips and gotchas
- Do not assume warm caches persist across physical node replacement. Always rely on a cache snapshot restore when moving tenant allocations.
- For tenants using private VPC endpoints, verify route table propagation and security group bindings after any terraform apply that touches attachments.
- Avoid full-scale thaw during global traffic peaks (US business hours 09:30–16:30 PT) unless there is a critical dependency.

Diagnostics cheat sheet (quick commands)
- Show node health: fleetctl node show NODE_ID
- List affected tenants: allocatorctl list-allocations --node NODE_ID
- Create reclaim tf-patch: ./tools/tf-patch --pool <POOL> --dec 1 --reason "reclaim" --out ./tfpatch
- Inspect NVML: nvidia-smi -i 0 -q | egrep "(ECC|Pending ECC)"

Templates and docs
- Incident title template: [GPU RECLAIM] <NODE_ID> <REGION> — <short reason>
- PR description checklist: include impact analysis, simulated plan output, rollout window, rollback plan, reviewer list.

Appendix: sample incident timeline (example)
- 00:00: Telemetry marks node offline (heartbeat loss)
- 00:04: Automated reclaim dry-run executed; stats show 3 tenants affected (cumulative 7% traffic)
- 00:10: Manual diagnostics: NVML errors observed -> hardware flagged; node marked RECLAIMED 00:22
- 00:25: TF patch created and PR opened; CI integration run (gpu-reclaim-integration) starts
- 01:10: PR merged during low-window; terraform apply reduces pool by 1 and provisions replacement node
- 03:00: Replacement joins; KV cache restore for tenant A begins
- 03:20: Tenant A pilot thaw 10% -> stable
- 04:30: Tenant A full thaw complete; billing unfrozen; incident closed after 24h audit

Contact list
- GPU Fleet Capacity oncall: gpu-capacity@pagerduty
- Hardware team: hw-support@redwood.internal
- Cloud Accounts: cloud-accounts@redwood.internal

Document history
- 2024-10-15: Created by Priya Natarajan
- 2024-11-02: Added terraform patch workflow (Ethan Park)
- 2025-02-01: Revised thaw ramp policy and rollout windows (Lina Martinez)

End of document
