title: GPU Inventory & Asset Management: IaC Catalog and Operational Playbook
preview:
Summary

This document describes the canonical inventory and asset management system for Redwood's GPU fleet, the associated Terraform / IaC modules, and the operational playbook for incident response, billing attribution, and reconciliation. It consolidates the authoritative source of truth, synchronization patterns, and runbook steps for recovery and manual changes.

Goals
- Provide a single source of truth for GPU devices, nodes, and reservations (gpu-inventory-svc).
- Ensure accurate cost and tag attribution for billing and chargebacks.
- Define IaC modules and usage patterns for lifecycle operations (provision, decommission, update).
- Document runbook for drift detection, reconciliation, and emergency manual recovery.

Audience
- Infra engineers owning GPU fleet provisioning and billing flows.
- SRE and Capacity teams responding to inventory drift or allocation issues.
- Finance/CostOps teams needing deterministic tagging and reporting.

Owner / Contacts
- Primary owner: eng-infra / Asha Patel (asha.patel@redwood.ai)
- Secondary: GPU Fleet team (gpu-fleet@redwood.ai)
- Pager: #oncall-gpu (Slack), rota: eng-infra oncall

Key services and modules
- gpu-inventory-svc (k8s service, Namespace: infra-gpu): authoritative API exposing /v1/devices, /v1/nodes, /v1/reservations.
- asset-syncer (cronjob in infra-tools): periodic job that reconciles cloud provider resources to the inventory DB.
- tf-module-gpu-inventory (Terraform module): manages cloud IAM, labels, and automated import hooks.
- asset-exporter (periodic job writing CSVs to s3://redwood-infra-gpu-inventory) used by CostOps.

Data model (schema excerpt)
- device_id (string): cloud provider GPU id (e.g., gcp-gpu-12345).
- node_id (string): host VM/container id.
- tenant_id (string): owning team or tenant identifier.
- allocation_state (enum): available | reserved | allocated | decommissioning.
- tags (map): canonical tags used for billing: project, cost_center, environment.

SLOs and tolerances
- Inventory sync lag: 99th percentile < 5 minutes (measured at asset-syncer -> gpu-inventory-svc end-to-end).
- Tag completeness: 99% of allocated GPUs must have project and cost_center tags populated at allocation time.
- Drift detection frequency: scan every 15 minutes; auto-reconcile for tag-only drift; alert for state mismatches.

IaC module catalog (summary table)
- tf-module/gpu-core: creates GCE/Azure GPU instance pools, node pools, and required IAM bindings.
- tf-module/gpu-labels: enforces canonical tagging and attaches label policies to instances.
- tf-module/gpu-asset-importer: helper that runs a one-time Terraform import for existing assets.
- tf-module/gpu-reservation: encapsulates reservation resources (monthly/commit instances) and billing tags.

Example Terraform usage (minimal)
```
module "gpu_core" {
  source = "git::ssh://git@repo.redwood.ai/terraform/modules/gpu-core.git//modules/gce"
  project = "redwood-prod"
  region  = "us-west1"
  node_pool_name = "gpu-pool-a"
  gpu_type = "nvidia-a100"
  gpu_count = 8
  labels = {
    project = "research"
    cost_center = "ml-research"
    environment = "prod"
  }
}
```

Importing an existing cloud GPU into inventory (one-time)
1) Run terraform import to bring the resource under tf control: 
   terraform import module.gpu_core.google_compute_instance.gpu vm-1234 
2) Tag the instance with canonical labels with the tf-module-gpu-labels module.
3) Trigger asset-syncer (ad-hoc) to force immediate reconciliation: kubectl -n infra-tools create job --from=cronjob/asset-syncer asset-sync-manual-$(date +%s)

Drift detection and auto-reconcile policy
- asset-syncer compares cloud API list -> inventory DB; diffs categorized as: tag-drift, state-drift, missing-resource.
- Tag-drift: auto-apply labels via cloud API if safe (no conflicting owner); log change and annotate in inventory with audit:auto-tagged.
- State-drift: if node shows as allocated in inventory but absent in cloud -> alert to oncall and mark allocation_state=investigating.
- Missing-resource: create incident ticket automatically after 2 consecutive scans.

Operational runbook: common incidents
1) Inventory sync lag exceeds SLO (99p > 5m)
   - Check asset-syncer job status: kubectl -n infra-tools get jobs; kubectl -n infra-tools logs job/asset-syncer-<id>
   - Verify cloud API throttling or 429 responses in logs. If throttling, escalate to CloudOps to request rate limit increase and scale syncer concurrency down to 2.
   - If DB write contention observed (RDS/Spanner): failover read replica per DB runbook.
2) Tag-completeness falls below 99%
   - Run reporter: python scripts/report_tag_completeness.py --window 24h; output CSV to s3.
   - For teams with missing tags, open auto-email to owners: costops@redwood.ai with summary.
   - For blocked allocations, block new allocations until tag fix if threshold > 10% for a tenant.
3) Allocation state mismatch: allocated but VM not present
   - Re-run asset-syncer immediately.
   - If still missing, check recent terraform apply history (CI/CD): ci-cd pipeline logs (Buildkite) for module.tf-module-gpu-core.
   - If suppression or accidental deletion suspected, follow recovery steps below.

Recovery steps for accidental deletion
- If VM/instance was deleted in cloud provider: check provider snapshots and backup window.
- If snapshot exists (<72 hours), create new instance from snapshot with same labels and run terraform import to re-associate.
- If no snapshot, mark device decommissioning and notify tenant; create replacement allocation from available pool.

Escalation matrix
- T1 oncall (eng-infra): first responder, initial triage, run the runbook steps.
- T2 GPU team lead: approve replacement allocations and billing adjustments.
- T3 CloudOps/SRE: for provider API issues or region-level outages.

Cost attribution and reporting
- canonical tags (project, cost_center) drive CostOps reports. Removal of tags requires approval and change logs.
- Monthly reconciliation job: asset-exporter runs daily and aggregates monthly cost buckets; saves to s3://redwood-finance/cost-reports/gpu/ and triggers notification to finance@redwood.ai.
- Chargeback rules: on-demand allocations billed by usage_hours * sku_rate; reserved instances amortized per reservation terms.

CI/CD patterns and safe deploys
- All Terraform changes to GPU modules must go through the tf-gpu pipeline (Buildkite) with the following checks:
  - plan must pass with zero unexpected deletions.
  - label policy validation (pre-commit hook) ensures required tags exist.
  - canary apply: apply to staging pool (staging-gpu-pool) then wait 30 minutes for inventory stability before applying to prod.
- Use terraform state locking with remote-state (s3 + dynamodb) and cross-account roles where appropriate.

Manual change governance (when IaC cannot handle emergency)
1) Record a one-off change in the change-log: github.com/redwood/infra-change-log (PR template).
2) Create a post-incident Terraform migration to reconcile manual change: include steps to import and codify the state, and add comments referencing the incident.

Auditability and security considerations
- All asset-syncer writes are auditable: each change includes actor, method (syncer | terraform | manual), and changelog_id.
- IAM: only the gpu-inventory-service account and CloudOps service account may modify allocation_state.
- Confidentiality: inventory contains tenant mappings; do not expose inventory exports externally. Use restricted S3 presigned links for external audits.

Operational maintenance checklist (quarterly)
- Validate label policy and update label dictionary.
- Run a reconciliation drill: intentionally flip allocation_state of 1% sample and run full reconcile; measure time to detect and resolve.
- Review Terraform modules for deprecation (GPU SKU changes) and create migration plans.

Appendix A: Useful commands and snippets
- Trigger ad-hoc asset-syncer: kubectl -n infra-tools create job --from=cronjob/asset-sync-$(date +%s)
- Query inventory API for untagged GPUs: curl -sS "https://gpu-inventory.internal/v1/devices?filter=missing_tags" | jq '.'
- Manual import (terraform): terraform import module.gpu_core.google_compute_instance.gpu <instance-id>

Appendix B: Example reconciliation SQL (redwood-internal)
```
SELECT device_id, node_id, allocation_state, tags->>'project' AS project
FROM gpu_inventory
WHERE allocation_state IN ('allocated','reserved')
  AND (tags->>'project' IS NULL OR tags->>'cost_center' IS NULL);
```

Related policies and pages
- See: eng-infra/terraform-and-iac/remote-state-segmentation-and-cross-account-locking-primer
- See: eng-infra/cloud-accounts-and-networking/gpu-network-topology-and-tenant-isolation-patterns

Revision history
- 2024-05-10: Created by Asha Patel (initial draft).
- 2024-09-03: Added auto-reconcile rules and SLOs (reviewed by Priya Gupta).
- 2025-01-12: Expanded CI/CD safe deploys and cost attribution sections (approved).


Importing an existing cloud GPU into inventory (one-time)
1) Run terraform import to bring the resource under tf control: 
   terraform import module.gpu_core.google_compute_instance.gpu vm-1234 
2) Tag the instance with canonical labels with the tf-module-gpu-labels module.
3) Trigger asset-syncer (ad-hoc) to force immediate reconciliation: kubectl -n infra-tools create job --from=cronjob/asset-syncer asset-sync-manual-$(date +%s)

Drift detection and auto-reconcile policy
- asset-syncer compares cloud API list -> inventory DB; diffs categorized as: tag-drift, state-drift, missing-resource.
- Tag-drift: auto-apply labels via cloud API if safe (no conflicting owner); log change and annotate in inventory with audit:auto-tagged.
- State-drift: if node shows as allocated in inventory but absent in cloud -> alert to oncall and mark allocation_state=investigating.
- Missing-resource: create incident ticket automatically after 2 consecutive scans.

Operational runbook: common incidents
1) Inventory sync lag exceeds SLO (99p > 5m)
   - Check asset-syncer job status: kubectl -n infra-tools get jobs; kubectl -n infra-tools logs job/asset-syncer-<id>
   - Verify cloud API throttling or 429 responses in logs. If throttling, escalate to CloudOps to request rate limit increase and scale syncer concurrency down to 2.
   - If DB write contention observed (RDS/Spanner): failover read replica per DB runbook.
2) Tag-completeness falls below 99%
   - Run reporter: python scripts/report_tag_completeness.py --window 24h; output CSV to s3.
   - For teams with missing tags, open auto-email to owners: costops@redwood.ai with summary.
   - For blocked allocations, block new allocations until tag fix if threshold > 10% for a tenant.
3) Allocation state mismatch: allocated but VM not present
   - Re-run asset-syncer immediately.
   - If still missing, check recent terraform apply history (CI/CD): ci-cd pipeline logs (Buildkite) for module.tf-module-gpu-core.
   - If suppression or accidental deletion suspected, follow recovery steps below.

Recovery steps for accidental deletion
- If VM/instance was deleted in cloud provider: check provider snapshots and backup window.
- If snapshot exists (<72 hours), create new instance from snapshot with same labels and run terraform import to re-associate.
- If no snapshot, mark device decommissioning and notify tenant; create replacement allocation from available pool.

Escalation matrix
- T1 oncall (eng-infra): first responder, initial triage, run the runbook steps.
- T2 GPU team lead: approve replacement allocations and billing adjustments.
- T3 CloudOps/SRE: for provider API issues or region-level outages.

Cost attribution and reporting
- canonical tags (project, cost_center) drive CostOps reports. Removal of tags requires approval and change logs.
- Monthly reconciliation job: asset-exporter runs daily and aggregates monthly cost buckets; saves to s3://redwood-finance/cost-reports/gpu/ and triggers notification to finance@redwood.ai.
- Chargeback rules: on-demand allocations billed by usage_hours * sku_rate; reserved instances amortized per reservation terms.

CI/CD patterns and safe deploys
- All Terraform changes to GPU modules must go through the tf-gpu pipeline (Buildkite) with the following checks:
  - plan must pass with zero unexpected deletions.
  - label policy validation (pre-commit hook) ensures required tags exist.
  - canary apply: apply to staging pool (staging-gpu-pool) then wait 30 minutes for inventory stability before applying to prod.
- Use terraform state locking with remote-state (s3 + dynamodb) and cross-account roles where appropriate.

Manual change governance (when IaC cannot handle emergency)
1) Record a one-off change in the change-log: github.com/redwood/infra-change-log (PR template).
2) Create a post-incident Terraform migration to reconcile manual change: include steps to import and codify the state, and add comments referencing the incident.

Auditability and security considerations
- All asset-syncer writes are auditable: each change includes actor, method (syncer | terraform | manual), and changelog_id.
- IAM: only the gpu-inventory-service account and CloudOps service account may modify allocation_state.
- Confidentiality: inventory contains tenant mappings; do not expose inventory exports externally. Use restricted S3 presigned links for external audits.

Operational maintenance checklist (quarterly)
- Validate label policy and update label dictionary.
- Run a reconciliation drill: intentionally flip allocation_state of 1% sample and run full reconcile; measure time to detect and resolve.
- Review Terraform modules for deprecation (GPU SKU changes) and create migration plans.

Appendix A: Useful commands and snippets
- Trigger ad-hoc asset-syncer: kubectl -n infra-tools create job --from=cronjob/asset-sync-$(date +%s)
- Query inventory API for untagged GPUs: curl -sS "https://gpu-inventory.internal/v1/devices?filter=missing_tags" | jq '.'
- Manual import (terraform): terraform import module.gpu_core.google_compute_instance.gpu <instance-id>

Appendix B: Example reconciliation SQL (redwood-internal)
```
SELECT device_id, node_id, allocation_state, tags->>'project' AS project
FROM gpu_inventory
WHERE allocation_state IN ('allocated','reserved')
  AND (tags->>'project' IS NULL OR tags->>'cost_center' IS NULL);
```

Related policies and pages
- See: eng-infra/terraform-and-iac/remote-state-segmentation-and-cross-account-locking-primer
- See: eng-infra/cloud-accounts-and-networking/gpu-network-topology-and-tenant-isolation-patterns

Revision history
- 2024-05-10: Created by Asha Patel (initial draft).
- 2024-09-03: Added auto-reconcile rules and SLOs (reviewed by Priya Gupta).
- 2025-01-12: Expanded CI/CD safe deploys and cost attribution sections (approved).

title: GPU cluster drain and traffic-shift handoff (capacity or degradation)
preview:
# Summary
This runbook describes how to drain an unhealthy or capacity-constrained GPU cluster and hand off the traffic shift to Platform/SRE routing controls. This is used when a single cluster is degraded (OOM storms, driver issues, bad nodes) and we need to move production inference traffic away with minimal disruption.

# When to use
- Single cluster GPU node failure rate spikes
- Elevated inference engine crashes in a cluster
- Cluster-level GPU saturation that cannot be mitigated via autoscaling quickly

# Preconditions
- Confirm impact scope: cluster vs region
- Identify affected cluster_id (e.g., usw2-a100-03)
- Confirm a target cluster/region exists with headroom

# Procedure
1) Freeze changes
   - Pause non-essential deployments in the affected cluster
   - Notify #eng-infra, #eng-platform, #eng-sre

2) Mark cluster as "draining"
   - Set scheduler to stop placing new allocations on cluster_id
   - Leave existing in-flight requests running when possible

3) Reduce admission
   - Enable stricter admission control for the cluster (if supported) to reduce queue growth

4) Coordinate routing shift
   - Ask Eng Platform to shift router weights away from the cluster or its region
   - Use: confluence://eng-platform/router-policy-manual-traffic-shift

5) Drain
   - Wait for in-flight requests to complete (target: < 15 minutes)
   - If cluster is unstable, perform a hard drain (terminate inference pods/instances)

6) Verify
   - Confirm router no longer sends new requests to cluster_id
   - Confirm target cluster/region is not saturating

7) Recovery
   - Replace bad nodes; roll driver/runtime updates if required
   - Run a small canary load before marking cluster back to "healthy"

# Rollback
- If the target cluster/region saturates, pause draining and request partial routing rollback

# Hand-off checklist
- Provide Platform/SRE: cluster_id, suspected cause, expected time to recover
- Provide incident channel: link to any relevant infra ticket

# Owners
- Eng Infra owns drain + fleet remediation
- Eng Platform owns router changes
- Eng SRE owns incident coordination

title: GPU Day-2 Configuration Templates and Rollforward Strategy
preview:
Overview

This page gathers: a set of standardized Day‑2 configuration templates for GPU node pools and inference runtime parameters, an operational decision tree favoring controlled rollforward (fix-forward) when safe, and a canary + load simulation harness pattern for CI/CD. The goal is predictable, low‑risk changes to GPU fleet behavior after initial provisioning (Day‑0), especially in high‑throughput inference clusters.

When to use this document
- Post-provisioning configuration changes (scaling policies, kernel/driver flags, cgroup/oom tuning, NVLink topology hints)
- Node image / runtime library patching where rollback is expensive or slow
- Changing batching / KV cache parameters that affect latency tail
- Upgrading autoscaler or spot fallback logic in Terraform modules

Key principles
- Prefer rollforward when the change is small, has an automatic remediation, or requires a state migration that rollback cannot undo (e.g., checkpoint reformatting).
- Canary at the smallest logical surface area (single AZ warm pool) with production traffic replayed locally.
- Run a simulated production load before promoting changes beyond canary.
- Maintain clear owner, SLO, and cost thresholds tied to the change.

Ownership and SLOs
- Owner: infra-gpu-ops (primary) / runtime-team (secondary)
- SLO targets (must be validated for the canary window):
  - P95 latency for 512 token requests <= 220ms
  - Error rate (5xx + model-fallbacks) <= 0.2%
  - Allocation success (GPU scheduling) >= 99.5% over 30m
- Cost guardrail: incremental per-hour cost increase for the fleet under experiment <= +12% (auto-fail if exceeded)

Quick decision tree (summary)
1. Is change reversible without state loss? If yes, default to rollback path.
2. Is change small and idempotent (config toggles, knob changes)? If yes, prefer rollforward with canary and automated reversion.
3. Does change require persistent migration (checkpoint format, KV index change)? If yes, plan rollforward—prepare migration, snapshot current KV/metric state, and create rollback plan that includes forward-fix options.

Canary pattern and load simulation harness
1) Canary surface: single warm‑pool (1–3 nodes), single AZ, single tenant with synthetic traffic mirror.
2) Traffic: replay last 1 hour of production token-level traces (anonymized) via the replay harness.
3) Metrics to validate: latency (P50/P95/P99), error rate, GPU util, OOM events, GPU temperature, KV cache hit rate.
4) Monitoring window: minimum 20m steady state after warmup.
5) Auto‑abort if any metric breaches configured thresholds (see Threshold table).

Threshold table (example)
| Metric | Canary threshold | Auto-abort condition | Owner action |
|---|---:|---|---|
| P95 latency (512 tokens) | 220ms | >220ms for 2 consecutive 10m windows | rollback or investigate kernel path |
| Error rate | 0.2% | >0.5% in 5m | abort canary, scale down canary nodes |
| GPU alloc success | >=99.5% | <98% | re-route traffic to fallback pool |
| KV cache hit rate | +/-5% relative | drop >10% | investigate mismatch in cache keys |
| Fleet cost delta | +12% | >12% increase sustained 30m | cancel promotion, evaluate batching change |

Day-2 Terraform module templates
The repo has three template variants: minimal, conservative, and performance‑first. Use the conservative template by default for live fleets. Below is a representative snippet for the autoscaler and warm pool definitions (HCL-style example):

```
module \"gpu_warmpool\" {
  source = \"git::ssh://git@repo.redwood/infrastructure/terraform-modules.git//gpu-warmpool?ref=v1.6\"

  name               = \"inference-warmpool-${var.cluster_suffix}\"
  instance_type      = var.instance_type
  min_size           = 2
  max_size           = 16
  warm_pool_size     = 2
  preemptible_policy = \"balanced\"    # options: balanced | spot-first | reserved-first

  lifecycle = {
    prevent_destroy = true
  }

  extra_user_data = file(\"${path.module}/templates/day2-bootstraps/${var.template}.sh\")
}
```

Note: day2-bootstraps include gpu-tuned sysctl, cgroup settings, and an init script that registers the node with the appropriate KV cache warmup service.

CI/CD flow (canary + promote)
1. Developer/infra engineer opens PR modifying module var or bootstrap script.
2. Pipeline runs: unit tests -> linter -> plan -> ephemeral apply to canary sandbox (namespace/canary cluster).
3. Integration step: traffic replay job executed against canary.
4. Prometheus checks run; if pass, human approval required (owner + oncall) for staged promote.
5. Promote executes staged Terraform apply that adds nodes in target region using rolling wave of NCC (node-change coordinator) to minimize evictions.

Example GitHub Actions stage (simplified YAML snippet):

```
jobs:
  canary-apply:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - name: terraform init
        run: terraform init -reconfigure
      - name: terraform plan
        run: terraform plan -out=tfplan
      - name: terraform apply to canary
        run: terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan -var='target=canary'
      - name: traffic replay
        run: |
          python infra/replay/replay.py --trace last-1h --target canary

  validate-canary:
    needs: canary-apply
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: wait and validate
        run: ./ci/validate_canary.sh --window 20m
```

Runbook: when canary fails (step by step)
1. CI auto-aborts and opens incident ticket tagged \"canary-failed\".
2. Oncall: examine canary metrics dashboard and logs.
3. If failure is resource transient (OOMs during warmup), scale canary up by X2 and re-run replay.
4. If deterministic (repro on second run), snapshot canary nodes (logs + nvprof traces) and escalate to runtime-team.
5. If migration-related (checkpoint version mismatch), run rollback if a safe snapshot exists; otherwise invoke rollforward: apply fix-forward migration job to canary and re-run validation.
6. Document RCA in the ticket and link to this page.

Rollforward recipe (when chosen)
- Preconditions: snapshot of KV and model checkpoints taken; automated drain/retry configured; rollback plan recorded but may be infeasible.
- Steps:
  1) Stage migration in canary only.
  2) Execute fix-forward automated script (example: checkpoint-rewrite --in-place --safe-backup=/snapshots/...)
  3) Run full traffic replay and cold-start tests.
  4) Monitor metrics for 2x canary window before expanding.
  5) Promote to a larger ring with pause points and human approvals.

Telemetry and observability checklist
- Dashboards: gpu-canary-overview (per-change), kv-cache-health, node-image-metrics, temperature-and-power
- Alerts (Prometheus): canary_failure_total > 0 OR p95_latency_increase_ratio > 1.15 for 10m -> pager (severity P1/P2 depending on impact)
- Logs: centralized nvprof/producer logs, system journal, k8s events, terraform apply audit

Operational templates (attachments)
- day2-templates/minimal.tf (example minimal knobs)
- day2-templates/conservative.tf (default for prod)
- ci/scripts/validate_canary.sh (validation helper)
- infra/replay/replay.py (trace anonymization + replay harness)

Appendix: safe-change checklist (must be completed and referenced in PR description)
1. Owner assigned and oncall notified.
2. Canary target and replay trace selected.
3. Backups & snapshots scheduled (KV, checkpoints).
4. Prometheus and Grafana panels linked in PR.
5. Rollback and rollforward scripts validated in staging.
6. Cost guardrails configured on the pipeline.

FAQ
Q: Why prefer rollforward vs rollback?
A: When changes mutate on-disk formats, caches, or checkpoint versions, rolling back can leave the fleet in a persistent incompatible state. A controlled fix-forward is safer in those cases.

Q: Who can approve a rollforward?
A: The change owner + runtime-team lead + infra oncall. If customer impact is high, include SRE manager.

Q: Are there automated safeties?
A: Yes — the CI pipeline enforces auto-abort thresholds and will revert Terraform state for canary applies if the auto‑abort condition triggers.

If you have additions to templates or new example scripts, please attach them to this page and add a reviewer tag.

Document history
- 2024-03-12: initial draft (Alejandra O.)
- 2024-05-07: added rollforward decision tree (Marcus Lin)
- 2024-09-08: updated thresholds and Prometheus alert names (Priya Desai)
